only this be the difference ; daily observation yield experiment of this diversity in place , whereas the word of God alone which endure for ever , give we the sure rule and ground of alteration in the event of different age . and yet in many remote place late make know unto the inhabitant of Europe , such strange event as antiquity have tell we , be sometime frequent in these country which we now inhabit , be not at this day altogether usual . and the face of time be now there muchwhat such , as the ancient register of time have portray unto we : as if the affair and fashion of this visible world , be frame accord to some invisible pattern or supercoelestiall character ; which vary their aspect in revolution of time , do now ( by reflect that force upon these remote country , which they do on these near adjoin in time past ) produce the like shape and fashion of thing there , as they have do here . and those place shall , in time , come to the self same temper and disposition which we now enjoy : and the posterity of such as now live there , shall hereafter suspect the undoubted story of our time concern their ancestor , as we do many ancient story of Jewry , Syria , Asia , or our own country , for want of like modern experiment in our land . 2 for the better rectify of our assent , which must be by the right balance of credulity and mistrust , it shall not be amiss to consider , that , beside these general diversity of time and place , particular kingdom and nation have their several age proportionable to infancy , youth , virility , and old age in man : nor be the period either of the whole age , or the several part thereof , one and the same in all , but vary in diverse kingdom , as the course of life , or several age do in diverse man . some kingdom bear age well unto a thousand ; some to six hundred year : other break and decay in half that time . again , as in the course of man life diversity of age require diverse manner or condition : so , in the same people or nation , some event be usual , as best befit they in that degree of their growth which answer to youth , or infancy , which seldom or never fall out in that part of their age which answer unto mature or old age in man , because not convenient for their constitution then ; and yet the want of like experience make they as distrustful and incredulous of what former have be , as old man be forgetful of their own disposition or temper in youth . And accord to the rule of physic : the ash of putrify body will soon inflame the humour hippoer . 6. epid . 2. et Merc. ad 2. de mor . : and therefore Tullus that artificial warrior , in articulate peace with the Albanes , put they in mind , that they ought not so much to settle present peace , as to provide for future agreement Dionys. 3. . and worthy do Isocrates reprove the grecian , because they do not compound , but delay enmity , until such time as one of they may destroy a other Isoc . in panegyr . . and excellent be that say of Cicero : pax est non in armis positis , at in abiecto omni armorum metu Cic. 10. lib . epist . Iam. epist . 6. . but to speak some what of the diversity of make peace , if this clause be comprehend in the article , that one of the party shall preserve the majesty and authority of the other with all kindness and faithfulness . doctor Baldus term this simplicem adhaerentiam Bal. 5. con . 106. , whereby ( as romanus expound it ) the weak part do adhere to the mighty : non ut subditi fiunt , sed ut defendantur Rom. con . 417. . this adherence be less than protection : and he that be a herent , or under protection , be not present under jurisdiction , but be only defensible from injury and violence , and that by soldier and armour : wherefore i wonder at alciat , when he say , Faederati latin loquentibus subditi sunt , non sotij Alcia . 7. con . 13. , aut adhaerente . neither be it any subjection , though they give somewhat in sign of superiority castal . de imp . q . 109. . neither be the carthaginian and Macedonian• subject to the roman , though they do pay unto they a yearly tribute . but if a league be once contract , it bind very strong and effectual ; and that certain ministery or dutiful respect be by reason of such league due and demandable , may evident appear by the league contract betwixt the roman and the lacedaemonian , wherein it be express , that they shall live accord to their own law , and shall not confer any thing in name of tribute , save only certain friendly ministery , and office Strabo lib . 8. . and for the further proof of the straitness and strength of this public bond , this form of league be find engrave in a ancient stone : batari fratres , and amici populi Romani dio lib . 69. : so that Bodinus be not to be hearken unto , who think that by friendship or league contract betwixt nation , no aid be due unless it be express mention body . lib . 5. the rep . c . vit . : for Baldus teach he otherwise , that there be one body of two city or two common weal , by reason of friendship conclude : Bal. con . 29 but for the better understanding hereof it be to be note that there be two kind of society or public friendship : one which be term nonlatinalphabet , the other which be name nonlatinalphabet . Suid. Thucyd. 1. Rhod. 11. a . le . 6. nonlatinalphabet , be when the party contract league be by force of the league to have the same friend , and the same enemy , which manner of society be much use by the roman : the other be but as it be the moiety or half part of this , as namely when one of the league be to help the other when he suffer injury : but not when he offer injury for it be a good rule in the civil law , rei turpis societa non intelligitur : l. 5. 7. ubi Bal. pro for . therefore he that covenant to defend a castle or farelet be not bind , if war be raise through his fault , to who he make the covenant : Alexand. 3. con . 114. and Castrensis avouch , that this aught to be understand of a necessary , not of a voluntary war : but this be clear and certain in this case , that he that be bind so to defend , be bind to defend by weapon : deci . li . 59 de . reg . iur . and 3. con . 117. and he that be bind in such case personal to help a other be much more bind to help he with money Alci . 3. consi 2. yet that must be think to be only then require at his hand , when the other can not provide for his own necessity . now it be to be consider , whether either of the party may depart from the league . and i think upon just occasion such a departure may be make : Faedus non violatur si ab eo disceditur ob rationem iustam l. 14. 15 16. pro soc . : but this must not be for a light cause : for light ▪ cause be always arise : and all contract will be most weak , if for a small and worthless cause it shall be break or not regard . but a prince may safe depart from the league if some part of the league be break by the other party : Cagnol . l. 41. Cod. de . trans . and league as all other contract be indiuidua deci . Cons. 265. cep . 455. 461. : for there be always exception understand in every league as these for example ; nisi causa superueniat : nisi culpa accesserit eius cvi promissio ista fit , and pactio foederis : rebus sic stantibus . thus we have speak of war and peace general , now it remain to discourse of the particular circumstance of these two principal point and moment of a common weal .
